relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Hazor's king gathers northern rulers as news of Israel spreads.
Hill, valley, lowland, and coast become one coalition map.
Peoples from east, west, mountain, and Hermon are drawn into the war.
The gathered army is pictured as sea sand with overwhelming horses and chariots.
The coalition camps together at Merom.
Joshua is told not to fear and is commanded to disable horses and burn chariots.
Israel arrives suddenly at the waters.
The coalition breaks beneath pursuit stretching toward Sidon and Mizpeh.
Horses are maimed and chariots burned.
Joshua turns toward Hazor, head of the northern kingdoms.
Every breathing life in Hazor is killed, and the city burns.
The same destruction spreads through the allied cities and kings.
Most mound-cities remain standing, though Hazor alone is burned.
Animals and goods are taken while human life is erased.
Joshua's obedience is narrated as unbroken continuity from Moses.
The conquered geography expands across hill, southland, lowland, and Arabah.
Kings from Seir's edge to Lebanon are captured and killed.
The summary admits the war lasted many days.
Only Gibeon survives through peace, and even that survival remains subordinated.
The enemies' resistance is attributed to Divine hardening so destruction can proceed without favor.
Anakim communities in the hill country are destroyed with their cities.
Only coastal remnants remain in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod.
The victor's narrative calls the land at rest after prolonged annihilation.

contemplative_note: Joshua 11 calls the aftermath 'rest,' but the chapter itself records burned Hazor, maimed horses, destroyed peoples, and no diplomatic mercy. Ethical reading must ask whose rest is being named and whose silence has been mistaken for peace.
